title: Difference Array
description: The mirror-image trick of Prefix Sum -- applying many range UPDATES efficiently instead of answering many range sum QUERIES.
objectives: Understand difference array as the inverse problem of prefix sum
Apply a range update in O(1) using a difference array
Reconstruct the final array with a prefix sum pass

sections.text: Prefix Sum solved: many range-sum QUERIES on a fixed array. Difference array solves the opposite kind of problem: many range UPDATES ("add 5 to every element from index 2 to 5") applied to an array, where we only care about the FINAL result after all updates.
Each direct update is O(range length). Applying q such updates costs O(q * n) in the worst case -- exactly the same shape of problem prefix sum solved, just for updates instead of queries.
An auxiliary array where each position holds the DIFFERENCE between consecutive elements of the original array, allowing a range update to be applied in O(1) by only touching the two endpoints.

diff[left] += value marks 'start adding value from here.' diff[right + 1] -= value marks 'stop adding it after here.' Each range update touches only TWO positions -- O(1) -- no matter how large the range is. The final prefix-sum pass (recall Prefix Sum's exact running-total technique) reconstructs the true values in one O(n) pass.

sections.title: Tracing the reconstruction
steps: Final result: [0, 5, 8, 8, 3, 3] -- matches applying both updates directly!

sections.text: Starting from all zeros: adding 5 to [1,3] gives [0,5,5,5,0,0]. Then adding 3 to [2,5] gives [0,5,8,8,3,3]. This matches the difference array's reconstructed result exactly -- confirming the technique.

sections.title: Direct range update vs difference array, for q updates on n elements
Verifying by hand
points: This matches the difference array's reconstructed result exactly

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the diff array needs ONE EXTRA slot (size n+1) to safely mark right+1 even when right is the last valid index.
Forgetting the final reconstruction step -- the diff array alone is NOT the answer; you must run a prefix-sum pass over it to get final values.
Mixing up which index gets +value and which gets -value -- it's always diff[left] += value and diff[right + 1] -= value.
Using a difference array for a problem that also needs range-sum QUERIES between updates -- difference array only helps when you can apply ALL updates first and read the final result afterward.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does a difference array solve?
questions.answer: Applying many range updates (adding a value to every element in a range) efficiently -- each update costs O(1) by only touching two positions, instead of O(range length) to update every element directly.

questions.question: How do you apply a range update [left, right] with value v using a difference array?
questions.answer: diff[left] += v and diff[right + 1] -= v -- marking where the addition starts and where it should stop, without touching anything in between.

questions.question: How do you get the final array values from a difference array?
questions.answer: Run a prefix sum pass over the difference array itself -- result[i] = result[i-1] + diff[i] -- which reconstructs the cumulative effect of every applied update.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A difference array is the mirror image of prefix sum: instead of answering range-sum queries quickly, it applies range UPDATES quickly. Adding a value v to every element in [left, right] only requires diff[left] += v and diff[right + 1] -= v -- O(1) per update, regardless of range size. After all updates are applied, a single prefix-sum pass over the difference array reconstructs the final values in O(n). This turns q range updates from O(q*n) worst case down to O(q + n) total, as long as you only need the final result after all updates, not intermediate query answers.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Difference array = mark the START (+value) and the STOP (-value) of an effect, then let a prefix-sum pass 'fill in' everything in between. Prefix Sum answers range QUERIES fast; Difference Array applies range UPDATES fast -- mirror images of each other.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What problem does a difference array solve, and how does it relate to prefix sum?
How do you apply a range update [left, right] with value v to a difference array?
How do you reconstruct final array values from a difference array?
Why does the difference array need one extra slot beyond the original array's size?

sections.title: Corporate Flight Bookings
sections.problemStatement: There are n flights labeled 1 to n. You are given an array of bookings where bookings[i] = [firsti, lasti, seatsi] means seatsi seats were reserved for flights firsti through lasti (inclusive) for every booking. Return an array answer of length n, where answer[i] is the total number of seats reserved on flight i+1.
hints: Each booking is a range update: add seatsi to every flight from firsti to lasti -- exactly this lesson's core operation.
Remember bookings are 1-indexed (flight 1 to n); the difference array can use 0-indexing internally with a small offset adjustment.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every booking, directly add seats to every flight in its range.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Use a difference array: mark +seats at first-1 and -seats at last, then reconstruct with a prefix sum pass -- exactly this lesson's technique.

sections.title: Car Pooling
sections.problemStatement: There is a car with capacity empty seats. Given trips where trips[i] = [numPassengersi, fromi, toi] indicates that the ith trip has numPassengersi passengers boarding at fromi and alighting at toi, return true if it's possible to pick up and drop off all passengers without exceeding capacity at any point.

examples.explanation: Between locations 3 and 5, both trips overlap: 2+3=5 passengers exceed capacity 4.

hints: Each trip is a range update: add numPassengersi passengers from fromi up to (but NOT including) toi -- the passenger gets off exactly at toi.
After reconstructing passenger counts at every location, check if any point exceeds capacity.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every trip, directly add passengers to every location in its range, then scan for any location exceeding capacity.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Use a difference array: +num at 'from', -num at 'to' (since the passenger leaves exactly at 'to'), then reconstruct with a prefix sum pass, checking capacity as we go.

sections.title: Range Addition
sections.problemStatement: You are given an integer length and an array of update queries. For each update[i] = [startIdx, endIdx, inc], increment all elements arr[startIdx..endIdx] (inclusive) by inc. Return the modified array after performing all the updates.
hints: This IS the difference array pattern in its purest, most direct form -- exactly the technique this lesson builds from scratch.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each update, directly add inc to every index in its range.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Apply each update as a difference array operation (+inc at start, -inc at end+1), then reconstruct with one prefix sum pass.

sections.title: Zero Array Transformation I
sections.problemStatement: You are given an integer array nums of length n and a 2D array queries, where queries[i] = [li, ri]. For each query, decrement the values at all indices from li to ri (inclusive) by at most 1. Return true if it's possible to transform nums into a zero array (all zeros) after processing ALL queries in order, false otherwise.

examples.explanation: One decrement across [0,2] turns [1,0,1] into [0,-1,0]... but decrementing by AT MOST 1 per query lets us choose to decrement only indices 0 and 2, giving [0,0,0].

hints: Each query can decrement each covered index by at most 1 -- so the MAXIMUM possible total decrement at index i is exactly how many queries cover it.
Use a difference array to count how many queries cover each index (a range update of +1 per query), then check that count >= nums[i] for every index.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each query, directly increment a coverage-count array across its range, then compare against nums.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Use a difference array to count query coverage per index in O(queries.length + n), then compare against nums.

sections.title: Zero Array Transformation II
sections.problemStatement: You are given an integer array nums and a 2D array queries where queries[i] = [li, ri, vali]. Each query decrements the values at indices li to ri (inclusive) by AT MOST vali (each index independently choosing any amount from 0 to vali). Return the minimum number of queries (processed as a prefix of the queries array, in order) needed to convert nums into a zero array, or -1 if it's impossible using all of them.

examples.explanation: Using only queries[0] ([0,2,1]) gives each index at most 1 total decrement capacity -- not enough for nums[0]=2. Using both queries[0] and queries[1] (both [0,2,1]) gives index 0 and index 2 a combined capacity of 2 each, which is exactly enough.

hints: This extends Zero Array Transformation I: instead of checking ALL queries at once, binary search (Binary Search lesson) on the NUMBER of queries used, since using more queries only ever helps (monotonic).
For a candidate count k, use a difference array to sum the max possible decrement (vali) each index can receive from the first k queries, then check every index's requirement is met.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every possible number of queries k from 1 to queries.length, rebuild the coverage using a fresh difference array and check feasibility.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Binary search on the number of queries k -- feasibility is monotonic (more queries never hurts), so find the smallest k that works using the same difference-array feasibility check as a helper.
